coredata.dc:title: In situ technique for synthesizing multiple ceramic particulates reinforced titanium matrix composites (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, D.
Lu, W.J.
Yang, Z.F.
Qin, J.N.
Zhang, D.
coredata.dc:description: In situ synthesized titanium matrix composites reinforced with multiple ceramic particulates including TiB, TiC and Y2O3 were fabricated by non-consumable arc-melting technique utilizing the chemical reaction among Ti, B2O3, B4C and Y. The thermodynamic feasibility of the in situ reactions has been considered. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to identify the phases in the composites. Microstructures of the composites were observed by means of optical microscope (OM), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and electron probe. It is concluded that multiple reinforcements are synthesized and they show different shapes: TiB grows in needle shape; TiC grows in near-equiaxed and rod-like shapes; Y2O3 grows in near-equiaxed shapes when the content of Y is 0.6wt.% and grows in dendritic shapes when the content of Y increases to 1.8wt.%. Reinforcements TiB, TiC and Y2O3 are distributed uniformly in the titanium matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium matrix composites
In situ
Thermal analysis
Microstructure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The changes of Gibbs free energy ΔG as a function of temperature for reaction (1).
The optical micrographs of (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti composites and pure titanium. (a) Sample 1; (b) sample 2; (c) sample 3; (d) pure titanium.

(a) BSE image of (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti composites and X-ray dot maps of elements (b) Y, (c) B, (d) C and (e) Ti (color level represents the intensity of the corresponding element). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)
SEM micrographs of Y2O3: (a) near-equiaxed shape; (b) dendritic shape.

article.ce:title: In situ technique for synthesizing multiple ceramic particulates reinforced titanium matrix composites (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, School of Materials Engineering, 1954 Huashan Road, Shanghai 200030, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In situ synthesized titanium matrix composites reinforced with multiple ceramic particulates including TiB, TiC and Y2O3 were fabricated by non-consumable arc-melting technique utilizing the chemical reaction among Ti, B2O3, B4C and Y. The thermodynamic feasibility of the in situ reactions has been considered. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to identify the phases in the composites. Microstructures of the composites were observed by means of optical microscope (OM), scanning electron microscope (SEM) and electron probe. It is concluded that multiple reinforcements are synthesized and they show different shapes: TiB grows in needle shape; TiC grows in near-equiaxed and rod-like shapes; Y2O3 grows in near-equiaxed shapes when the content of Y is 0.6wt.% and grows in dendritic shapes when the content of Y increases to 1.8wt.%. Reinforcements TiB, TiC and Y2O3 are distributed uniformly in the titanium matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium matrix composites (TMCs) discontinuously reinforced with ceramic particulates have significant potential for structural applications due to their outstanding combination of high specific strength, elevated temperature resistance and stiffness as well as low density. These properties have made particulate-reinforced TMCs an attractive candidate for use in aerospace, transportation and industrial sectors. Moreover, in situ technologies have attracted more attention as a method to prepare discontinuous-reinforced TMCs because of the ease of fabrication and low cost [1]. Usually, self-propagation high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [2,3], mechanical alloying (MA) [4,5], powder metallurgy (PM) [6,7], rapid solidification powder processing (RSP) [8,9] and ingot metallurgy technique (IM) [10,11] have been used to produce TMCs by in situ processing techniques.

Reinforcement materials generally used to reinforce titanium alloys include SiC, TiC, Al2O3, TiB and TiB2. According to the literature, extensive works have been conducted on the unitary or binary ceramic particulates reinforced TMCs such as TiB/Ti or (TiC+Al2O3)/Ti, but few is concerning multiple particulates reinforced TMCs [1]. Multiple reinforcements growing in different shapes and sizes are formed in situ by chemical reaction between the element constituents of composites. Owing to the interactions between the different reinforcements, multiple reinforced TMCs may possess some new performance and unique mechanical properties that are difficult to obtain in conventional unitary or binary ceramic particulates reinforced composites.

The paper explores and succeeds in fabricating multiple ceramic particulates (TiB+TiC+Y2O3) reinforced TMCs by common casting technique of titanium alloys. Ultrafine ceramic particulate reinforcements are formed in situ by the chemical reaction among Ti, B2O3, B4C and rare earth metal Y. The phase constitution and microstructure of the novel composites are evaluated in the paper.

Selection of a compatible reinforcement is very important for developing particulate reinforced TMCs. Various reinforcements are used in TMCs including SiC, Al2O3, TiC and TiB and TiB2. Among these reinforcements, TiB has been identified as the best reinforcement for TMCs due to its excellent capabilities, such as the thermal stability at high temperature, the high elastic modulus and a similar density to titanium [7,13]. With the characters of high hardness, high modulus and rather high flexural strength, TiC was a suitable candidate to be used as a reinforcement phase for wear resistant TMCs [12,13]. The rare earth metal Y added to the matrix can greatly increase instantaneous intensity of the matrix alloy at high temperature because the rare earth oxide particulates combined with rare earth metal and oxygen in the matrix alloy are beneficial to refine crystal grain, increase fatigue resistance and improve thermal stability [14]. Based on the above factors, multiple ceramic phases TiB, TiC and Y2O3 were in situ synthesized to obtain novel TMCs. It is expected that the in situ synthesized multiple ceramic reinforcements will grow in different shapes and sizes, which ranges from several micrometers to nanometers.

In this work, multiple ceramic phases TiB, TiC and Y2O3 are synthesized by the following reaction:
(1)
7 Ti +
B 4
C +
B 2
O 3
+ 2 Y = 6 TiB + TiC +
Y 2
O 3

Furthermore, the paper has considered the thermodynamic feasibility of the in situ reactions involved in fabricating in situ composites. ΔG determines the reaction feasibility. ΔG is defined as:
(2)
Δ
G =
Δ
H − T
Δ
S

The change of Gibbs free energy versus temperature for reaction (1) is plotted in Fig. 1 . ΔG is negative for the temperature ranges investigated, indicating that the reaction for the formation of TiB, TiC and Y2O3 is feasible thermodynamically. Namely TiB, TiC and Y2O3 reinforcements can be synthesized by the reaction (1) during heating.

Sponge titanium (>99.5%, purity), B2O3 powder (99% purity), B4C powder (99% purity) and rare earth metal Y ingot (99% purity) were used as raw material. In order to investigate the morphologies of reinforcements with different yttrium contents in the titanium matrix alloy, three kinds of samples were fabricated. Unreinforced Ti sample was also fabricated under the identical conditions. The weight percentage of reactants and theoretical volume percentage of the products are listed in Table 1 . Stoichiometric amounts of raw materials were blended and melted in a non-consumable vacuum arc-melting (VAR) furnace. In order to ensure the chemical homogeneity of the composites, the ingots were melted at least three times.

The samples for optical microscopy (OM) were cut from the ingots. Then the OM samples were prepared using conventional grinding and mechanical polishing techniques. The polished samples were etched in Kroll's reagent (composition: 1–3ml HF, 2–6ml HNO3, 100ml water). Phase identification of the composites was performed by a D/max 2550V X-ray Diffraction Analysis (XRD). The characters of reinforcements were observed by LEICA MEF4A/M optical microscope, Philips XL30W/TMP scanning electron microscope (SEM) and JXA-8100 electron probe microanalyzer.

Fig. 2 shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the composites investigated. Ti, TiB, TiC and Y2O3 diffraction peaks can be observed in the XRD patterns. This demonstrates that in situ reaction between Ti, B4C, B2O3 and Y has taken place according to the formula (1), and the novel TMCs reinforced with TiB, TiC and Y2O3 ceramic particulates have been produced by a non-consumable arc-melting technology. It is evident from Fig. 2 that there are no significant differences in the XRD patterns except the difference in the peak intensities, which resulted from an increase in the volume percent of reinforcements.

Fig. 3 shows the optical micrographs of (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti composites fabricated. It can be observed that reinforcements are distributed uniformly in the titanium matrix. Compared with unreinforced titanium, crystal grains of (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti composites are obviously finer. The melting point of in situ synthesized ceramic phases is higher than that of titanium alloy, so they will precipitate first from the titanium matrix. It is clear that in situ synthesized multiple reinforcements can act as nucleating center, resulting in refining the grain size of the matrix alloy.

In Fig. 3, the morphologies of reinforcements exhibit different shapes: needle, near-equiaxed, rod-like and dendritic shapes. The results of energy dispersion X-ray spectrometer (EDS) from Fig. 4 show: needle shaped reinforcements are TiB; near-equiaxed and rod-like shaped reinforcements are TiC; and Y2O3 particulates show near-equiaxed and dendritic shapes which are obviously bigger than TiB or TiC. Moreover, the SEM images shown in Fig. 5 are used to characterize the microstructure of Y2O3.

According to Figs. 3 and 5, the Y2O3 particulates in titanium matrix are small and show near-equiaxed shapes, reaching diameters of approximate 10μm, when the content of yttrium is about 0.6wt.% (Fig. 3a). There is a corresponding coarsening of Y2O3 particulates when the content of yttrium increases. The Y2O3 particulates show obvious dendritic morphology when yttrium content increases to 1.8 wt.%. The Y2O3 particulates grow much larger, reaching lengths of approximate 60μm in some cases (Fig. 3b and c).

The different shapes of the reinforcements are related to their crystal structures. TiB has a B27 structure with lattice parameters a =0.612, b =0.306, and c =0.456nm. TiB grows faster along the (010) direction than normal to the (101) and (100) planes, thus leading to a needle morphology [16–18]. TiC exhibits a NaCl-type structure. The position of Ti and C atoms in the lattice is center-symmetric. Thus, when TiC nucleates, the growth rate in symmetric crystal planes is similar, leading to the formation of equiaxed spherical particulates [19,20]. The Y2O3 has a Mn2O3 type structure with a lattice parameter a =10.60187nm, so the crystal structure of Y2O3 shows high geometry symmetry in the chemical bond between the atoms O and Y. It indicates that Y2O3 will grow at the same speed in each direction and form equiaxed or dendritic shape. When Y2O3 grew from the melt, it easily formed constitutional supercooling during the solidification process. Since the growth of Y2O3 was not constrained, Y2O3 grew into a coarse dendritic shape [21,22].

ce:list-item.ce:para: In situ synthesized multiple ceramic particulates reinforced titanium matrix composites (TiB+TiC+Y2O3)/Ti can be fabricated by common casting technique utilizing the chemical reaction among Ti, B4C, B2O3 and rare earth metal Y.
Reinforcements TiB, TiC and Y2O3 are distributed uniformly in the titanium matrix.

Reinforcements grow in different shapes and sizes: TiB grows in needle shape; TiC grows in near-equiaxed and rod-like shapes; Y2O3 grows in near-equiaxed shapes when the content of Y is 0.6wt.% and grows in dendritic shapes when the content of Y increases to 1.8wt.%. The difference in the morphologies of reinforcements is related to their different crystal structure.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge a financial support provided by A Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China under Grant No. 200332, Major Fundamental Research Project of Shanghai Science and Technology Committee under Grant No. 04DZ14002, 04QMX1412, 0452nm045, Aeronautics Foundation of China under Grant No. 03G57007 and ItoYama Foundation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Advances in Titanium Metal Matrix Composites
Thermochemical Data of Elements and Compounds
